era_context: The early Iron Age Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The Neo-Assyrian Empire is in its formative phase under Ashur-dan II (r. 934–912 BCE). The Kingdom of Israel under the United Monarchy has recently fragmented (c. 930 BCE) into Israel (north) and Judah (south). Egypt is in the 21st Dynasty, a period of declining central authority. The Phoenician city-states (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos) are expanding maritime trade networks.

events.title: Reign of Ashur-dan II of Assyria (traditional dating begins)
events.description: Ashur-dan II is traditionally dated to have begun his reign around 934 BCE (some sources place events within his reign in the 980s BCE range). He is credited with military campaigns to restore Assyrian territorial control and prestige after a period of decline. His reign marks the beginning of the Neo-Assyrian expansion, though the precise chronology of individual campaigns remains debated among scholars.

sources.name: Assyrian King List and annals (later compilation from cuneiform sources)
Synchronistic History (cuneiform text correlating Assyrian and Babylonian rulers)

events.certainty_note: Absolute dating of early Neo-Assyrian reigns depends on synchronisms with Egyptian and Babylonian chronologies, which themselves have ±10–20 year uncertainties in this period. The 934 BCE date is conventional but not firmly anchored to astronomical events.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of the early Neo-Assyrian period (10th–9th centuries BCE) is contested. Some scholars argue for a 'low chronology' that would shift these dates forward by 50–100 years; others defend the 'middle chronology' used here. No specific events are reliably attested to the year 984 BCE itself; this entry reflects the conventional placement of Ashur-dan II's reign within a broader period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for Mesopotamia in the 10th century BCE is heavily dependent on Assyrian royal inscriptions, which are propagandistic and often lack precise dating. Egyptian sources for this period are sparse and fragmented. The absence of contemporary written records from the Levantine kingdoms (Israel, Judah, Phoenicia) for specific years in this decade means that synchronization with Mesopotamian chronology is indirect and uncertain. Archaeological evidence (stratigraphy, pottery, radiocarbon dating) is improving but remains coarse-grained for year-by-year precision.

graph_edges.note: Ashur-dan II's reign spans approximately 934–912 BCE in conventional chronology
